excludeReasons.reason: Training curriculum (questions.ts, modules.ts) embeds intentional vulnerable examples — sample SSNs, weak-crypto snippets — as teaching content. The scanner flags its own example material, not product runtime. Excluded from the self-scan.
LLM rule definitions and prompts embed example vulnerable code (DES, weak hashing, auth-bypass) so the model learns what to detect. Detection content, not runtime code.
Rule catalog metadata. The scanner matches its own rule titles/descriptions (e.g. 'Missing MFA for PHI Access'). Detection content, not runtime code.

acknowledgedFindings.reason: Scanner source code contains intentional example patterns for detecting PHI exposure, weak encryption, and security vulnerabilities. These are demonstration patterns used for detection logic, not actual production code.
Fixer code intentionally contains before/after examples of vulnerable code patterns for transformation strategies. These are fix templates used to demonstrate remediation, not production code.

Reporter code handles and displays finding data which may contain PHI-like strings in examples and remediation guides. This is necessary for report generation and documentation, not actual PHI or production code.
Test files contain intentional vulnerable code examples to verify scanner detection capabilities and ensure comprehensive coverage of security patterns.
Package lock file may contain references to cryptographic algorithms in dependency names or metadata. These are dependency references, not actual usage in production code.
Example configuration file contains sample patterns including HTTP URLs for demonstration purposes. This is documentation, not production configuration.
Stack detection guides contain example code snippets and configuration patterns for various frameworks. These examples may include security anti-patterns for educational purposes.
CLI help/template guidance text (e.g. 'obtain signed acknowledgment', 'patient notice') printed to the console — user-facing output, not PHI written to error logs.

Hardcoded demo-animation data: the FAKE_FILES and FINDING_TITLES arrays render a simulated scan. Strings like 'Weak MD5 hash for passwords', 'No session timeout configured', and 'src/models/patient.ts' are display literals, not real auth/crypto/PHI code.
Matches the Stripe Checkout Session API (stripe.checkout.sessions.create / session.id / session.url) — a payment session, not an authentication session-timeout control.
Matches the Stripe webhook event 'checkout.session.completed' and the Stripe.Checkout.Session object — a payment session, not an authentication session.
Matches stripe.billingPortal.sessions.create — a billing-portal session, not an authentication session-timeout control.
Matches the string literal 'Error creating checkout session' in a client-side alert; refers to Stripe checkout, not an authentication session.
Legal/privacy prose describing Supabase Auth session cookies; documentation copy, not a session-timeout configuration.
persistSession:false on the server-side Supabase client is the stateless/secure setting; the rule keyword-matches 'Session'. There is no session-timeout gap here.

GitHub App machine-to-machine JWT (jwt.sign with exp = now + 600, GitHub's 10-minute maximum) used to mint installation tokens — not a PHI-bearing user session. SESSION/REVOKE keyword-match jwt.sign; revocation is moot for a 10-minute app JWT.
select('*') targets the dashboard's own metadata tables (projects/scans/findings), which store compliance-scan results, not patient PHI; the minimum-necessary-PHI rule is not applicable.
Heuristic fired on a generic CRUD catch block; breach-notification is an organizational/process control, not something implemented per API route.
supabase.auth.signUp creates the account; MFA is enrolled via Supabase's separate MFA flow post-signup, not configured at this call. The rule matches the auth-call keyword.
